items.title: Audit trail for your Elastic Cloud organization
items.body:
**What the feature is and who is it for**
This feature introduces organization-wide audit logging that can be easily enabled directly from the Elastic Cloud Console UI. It’s designed for Elastic Cloud administrators and security teams who need full visibility into activity across their organization — whether through the Web UI or API — to strengthen governance, compliance, and operational oversight.

Note that this initial outcome focuses exclusively on auditing administrative interactions related to Elastic Cloud Hosted resources (i.e. deployments) and organization-level changes (e.g., member management, API keys, etc).

**Value proposition**
With this capability, Elastic Cloud administrators gain a centralized, searchable audit trail of all key user actions — from configuration changes and security updates to access management and resource modifications. This visibility helps teams quickly answer questions like “Who modified this deployment’s resources?” or “When was this API key last used?”, improving accountability, troubleshooting, and security posture across their environment.

fieldValues.Title: Audit trail for your Elastic Cloud organization

summary.for: Government IT administrators, security officers, and compliance teams managing Elastic Cloud deployments in regulated public sector environments
summary.value: Strengthens compliance posture and accountability by providing a centralized, searchable audit trail of all administrative actions — supporting FISMA, FedRAMP, and zero-trust governance requirements
summary.scope: Organization-wide audit logging via Cloud Console UI or API covering deployment changes, member management, API key activity, and access modifications

items.title: AWS PrivateLink support in Elastic Cloud Serverless
items.body:
**What the feature is and who it's for:** This is a new capability allowing customers to establish secure, private network connections to their Serverless projects, using AWS PrivateLink, to ensure data traffic bypasses the public internet. This feature is designed for enterprise customers, particularly those in regulated industries or with strict corporate security policies that prohibit data traversal over public networks. Cloud administrators can configure these private endpoints and use VPC filtering to control access from specific virtual private clouds. The setup is managed directly within the Elastic Cloud console for a seamless administrative experience.

**Value proposition:** The primary value of this feature is a significantly enhanced security and compliance posture for sensitive workloads running on Elastic's serverless platform. By eliminating public internet exposure, it drastically reduces the attack surface and helps organizations maintain a zero-trust network architecture. This capability unblocks the adoption of Elastic Cloud Serverless for enterprises that were previously hesitant due to stringent data governance requirements. It allows organizations to fully leverage the operational efficiency and scalability of a serverless architecture without compromising on enterprise-grade security. Ultimately, this solidifies Elastic Cloud Serverless as a secure-by-design platform, empowering customers to confidently run their most critical applications in the cloud.

fieldValues.Title: AWS PrivateLink support in Elastic Cloud Serverless

summary.for: Government and defense cloud architects, security officers, and program managers operating sensitive workloads in AWS GovCloud or regulated environments
summary.value: Eliminates public internet exposure for Elastic Cloud Serverless traffic, strengthening zero-trust network posture and supporting compliance with data sovereignty, FedRAMP, and DoD security requirements
summary.scope: AWS PrivateLink integration for Serverless projects with private endpoint configuration, VPC-level access filtering, and console-managed setup

items.title: More flexible CCS version compatibility between clusters
items.body:
Customers with multiple clusters connected together via CCS struggle with planning stack upgrades The current version compatibility model requires customers to upgrade the local cluster last. As a result, the central/local cluster might end up remaining on an older, potentially vulnerable version of the stack because remotes are not upgraded yet.

Customers solve this today by maintaining a dedicated cluster for cross-cluster search. Keeping this cluster on the earliest version needed to search the other clusters. For example, if you have 7.17 and 8.x clusters, you can maintain a dedicated 7.17 cluster to use as the local cluster for cross-cluster search. Or they keep each cluster no more than one minor version apart. This lets them use any cluster as the local cluster when running a cross-cluster search.

We would like to allow remote versions to diverge by more than v-1

fieldValues.Title: More flexible CCS version compatibility between clusters

summary.for: DOD and defense IT program managers and infrastructure teams operating multi-cluster Elasticsearch environments across classified or mission networks
summary.value: Reduces security risk by enabling timely patching and upgrades of central clusters without being blocked by remote cluster readiness, supporting continuous ATO and vulnerability management compliance
summary.scope: Expanded cross-cluster search (CCS) version compatibility allowing remote clusters to diverge beyond one minor version, reducing upgrade coordination constraints across distributed environments

items.title: Admins have insights about CCS queries health and performance via dashboard and alerts
items.body:
Customers with multiple remote clusters, prevalent in enterprise, government, and Security Solution users, using CCS need to offer a reliable and performant search/dashboard service to their users. To achieve that, they need visibility into CCS queries' health and performance.

Today, some customers make their own dashboards and find crafty solutions to gather some data on the CCS queries or remote cluster health. We know of one that has a scheduled python script and a watcher that runs sample queries against the remotes so they have status data to show in a CCS Stability dashboard they have created.

items.title: Fine-grained log delivery controls in Elastic Cloud
items.body:
**What the feature is and who it’s for**

This feature introduces flexible logging controls for Elastic Cloud users who want more granular control over how their Hosted deployment and Serverless project logs are managed. It’s designed for administrators and DevOps teams looking to reduce storage overhead, improve visibility, and align log management with their operational and compliance needs.

**Value proposition**

Elastic Cloud customers can now enable only the logs that matter and send them to the ideal destination within the same or a different CSP region — whether they care about **audit logs** for compliance, **query logs** for performance optimization, or **diagnostic logs** for deeper troubleshooting. This means fewer unnecessary logs taking up space, more targeted insights, and the ability to apply different retention policies per log type. In short: smarter logging, lower costs, and better control.

The EIS Tier view in AutoOps for Elastic Cloud Serverless provides visibility into how inference performance, and token usage, helping users understand how inference activities impact overall project efficiency and usage.

This capability is designed for Serverless users running inference workloads who want to track their token consumption, optimize performance, and manage costs more effectively.

items.title: AutoOps for Self Managed Clusters (Cloud Connected)
items.body:
**What the feature is and who it’s for**
AutoOps for self-managed clusters brings the same real-time diagnostics, performance tuning, and issue detection capabilities available in Elastic Cloud to customers running Elasticsearch on their own infrastructure. It helps enterprise administrators, DevOps, and SRE teams simplify cluster management, improve performance, and reduce hardware costs without deploying or maintaining separate monitoring systems.

This is the first in a roadmap of Elastic Cloud Connected Services for self-managed environments, enabling customers to use Elastic Cloud capabilities without local installation or management, while keeping all data within their own environment.

**Value proposition**
AutoOps delivers real-time diagnostics, tailored recommendations, and resource utilization insights directly to self-managed clusters—helping teams detect issues early, tune performance, and lower hardware costs. It identifies bottlenecks such as slow queries, shard imbalance, and inefficient mappings, and recommends specific actions to resolve them.

Elastic Support engineers have secure, read-only access to AutoOps diagnostics, enabling faster, more accurate support. Included with the Enterprise subscription at no additional cost, AutoOps reduces manual effort, improves stability, and simplifies running Elasticsearch at scale.

items.title: Draft Cases
items.body:
Introduces a "Draft" state for cases, providing a dedicated space for initial triage and evidence collection before a formal incident is declared.

**Value proposition**
Decouples the act of investigating from the act of declaring an incident. This allows teams to safely separate "signal" from "noise," ensuring that low-confidence investigations do not pollute critical metrics like Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) or trigger unintended automated responses.

**Expected outcome**
Users will be able to create and collaborate on Draft cases to validate findings. These cases can be seamlessly promoted to standard Cases once a threat is verified, or closed without impacting performance stats. This distinction will be exposed in the data schema to support filtered reporting and smarter automation logic.

fieldValues.Title: Draft Cases

summary.for: DOD and defense SOC teams, cyber incident response program managers, and mission owners requiring clean audit trails and defensible incident declaration processes
summary.value: Reduces false-positive incident noise to protect MTTR metrics and prevent unintended automated responses, supporting accurate reporting for compliance frameworks and inspector general or command-level oversight
summary.scope: Draft case state for pre-declaration triage and evidence collection, seamless promotion to formal incidents, schema-level differentiation enabling filtered reporting and automation logic

items.title: ES|QL: Effectively search unmapped data
items.body:
## Value Prop

Today it is very hard to access and analyze data that reside in unmapped fields as both `_search` and ES|QL require fields to be mapped in order to be used in queries. Having not fully mapped data more often happens with logs, which are often unstructured and composed of too many variations of fields. Users have to parse each field that they want to be able to search and analyze. 

With this effort, we enable users with data that is stored in Elasticsearch but might not be fully mapped to be able to access all fields in a similar way so that they can search and analyze unmapped data.

## Expected Outcome

ES|QL will allow accessing and searching unmapped fields.

summary.for: DOD and defense analysts, cyber operators, and mission owners ingesting high-volume, unstructured log and telemetry data
summary.value: Accelerates threat hunting and incident response by enabling full-fidelity search across all log data without requiring pre-mapping, reducing blind spots in security operations
summary.scope: ES|QL query support for unmapped fields, allowing analysis of unstructured and partially mapped data stored in Elasticsearch

items.title: Split view in Discover

items.title: Generate ES|QL with natural language in Kibana
items.body:
**What the feature is**

Generate ES|QL with natural language in Kibana. This feature will introduce an in-context Natural Language Processing (NLP) capability directly within the Kibana ES|QL editor. Users will be able to type a question in plain English, and the system will generate the corresponding ES|QL query, replacing their prompt in the editor.

**Value proposition**:

This feature helps both new and experienced users bypass the learning curve of the ES|QL query language. Currently, query creation can be slow, and existing AI assistant workflows require a disjointed, inefficient copy-and-paste action. By integrating Natural Language directly into the editor, users can simply type their intent, allowing the system to instantly generate a complex and validated ES|QL query. This provides a seamless, integrated experience that significantly accelerates data exploration and analysis for everyone.

**Expected outcome**:

- Type a question or command in plain English directly within the query editor.
- Trigger the generation using a simple keyboard shortcut (e.g., Cmd+Enter).
- The system will automatically generate and replace the plain language prompt with a valid ES|QL query, using the currently selected data source for context.
- Users can then immediately inspect, edit, and run the generated query.

items.title: Variable controls for index pattern in ES|QL (FROM ?index)
items.body:
**What the feature is** 

Variable controls for index pattern in ES|QL (FROM ?index)

**Value proposition**:

This feature targets users who create dashboards with ES|QL visualizations over data that is partitioned into multiple index patterns or streams. The main problem today is that users must create and maintain duplicate dashboards to run the same query logic against different data sources, leading to high maintenance overhead. This capability solves that by allowing a single dashboard to use a user-facing control (linked to the named parameter) to dynamically switch the entire dashboard's context to a different index pattern, significantly reducing duplication and simplifying maintenance.

**Expected outcome**:

- The ES|QL syntax will allow a named parameter to be set in the index pattern position of the FROM clause (e.g., FROM ?my_index).
- Users can link a dashboard control (like a static dropdown) to this named parameter.
- Selecting an index pattern or data stream from the control will dynamically update all linked ES|QL visualizations to query the selected data source.
- A single dashboard can now be used to visualize the same information across multiple, distinct data sources (e.g., daily indices, regional data streams).

fieldValues.Title: Variable controls for index pattern in ES|QL (FROM ?index)

summary.for: Government and defense data analysts, mission operations program managers, and SOC/intelligence dashboard administrators managing multi-domain or partitioned data environments
summary.value: Reduces duplicative dashboard infrastructure across classification levels, mission areas, or regional data streams—lowering maintenance burden and accelerating operator access to mission-relevant data within compliant Elastic deployments (FedRAMP, IL4/IL5)
summary.scope: Dynamic index pattern switching in ES|QL dashboards via named parameters (FROM ?index), enabling a single dashboard to query across multiple indices or data streams through a user-facing control

items.title: AI-Driven Entity Analytics
items.body:
**Background:**

Entity Analytics represent the key pieces of information involved in a security incident in a SIEM. They are the “who”, “what”, “where”, and “when” of any research, hunt or investigation providing crucial context for effective response. Entities are objects in every modern SIEM and can include users, hosts, files, network artifacts, threat intelligence, vulnerabilities and much more.

Threat hunting has traditionally been a highly manual process requiring deep expertise in adversary behaviors, query languages, infrastructure, users and security analysis. Most organizations lack dedicated threat hunters or the time required to perform regular hunting operations. Even when orgs have the resources, the process is inefficient and often reactive rather than proactive.

**What problem are we solving**

Among the personas that comprise our customers' security operations teams, many of them rely on complex processes and query languages in order to investigate and find threats and risky behaviors in their environments. Threat hunting activities, in particular, are in need of this specialized, technical knowledge on a regular basis, as these individuals who go hunting are proactive.

We can significantly impact and streamline threat hunting activities through natural language, by providing guided experiences based on the combination of generative AI, entity analytics data, curated threat intelligence, and the powerful search analytics capabilities provided by Elastic.

**Expected outcome**

Deliver AI-driven entity analytics, including historical entity store, NL-driven entity queries, entity resolution, NL-based visualizations, and more, to satisfy today’s SIEM requirement that was historically fulfilled by legacy UEBA vendors, to complement all analyst and threat hunting workflows, as well as the needs of insider threat teams.

**Roadmaps Included**
- Privilege User Monitoring
- Entity Risk Scoring Alert Filtering
- Entity GenAI Hightlights
- Historical Context Changes & Behavioral Baseline
- Watchlist
- Overview Homepage
- Entity Graph

fieldValues.Title: AI-Driven Entity Analytics

summary.value: Accelerates threat hunting and insider threat detection across classified and sensitive environments with AI-driven behavioral analytics, reducing reliance on scarce cyber expertise while strengthening zero-trust and continuous monitoring compliance postures
summary.scope: Historical entity store, natural language threat queries, entity risk scoring, privileged user monitoring, behavioral baselining, watchlists, entity graph, and AI-generated investigation highlights

items.title: Entity Analytics - Historical Context Changes & Behavioral Baseline
items.body:
**Problem**
To investigate effectively, analysts need immediate context on why a specific entity behavior was flagged as anomalous.

Using a "New Country Login" anomaly for user "John Doe" as an example:

Required Context: The analyst must immediately see the baseline behavior (e.g., "Baseline Country: USA") directly compared against the anomalous event (e.g., "Anomalous Login: Brazil").
Without this readily available comparative data, the analyst is forced to manually hunt for the user's behavioral history, which delays investigation and increases the mean time to resolution (MTTR).

**Value proposition**
Enable security analysts to conduct faster and more accurate threat hunts by providing them with the complete historical context and relationship mapping for entity and behavioral changes.

summary.for: DOD and defense agency SOC analysts, cybersecurity program managers, and insider threat program owners requiring rapid anomaly triage
summary.value: Accelerates insider threat and account compromise investigations by surfacing behavioral baselines alongside anomalies, reducing MTTR and strengthening Zero Trust identity monitoring posture
summary.scope: Automated historical context and baseline comparison surfaced directly within entity anomaly alerts, eliminating manual behavioral history lookups

items.title: Materialized Views: Accelerate queries by defining automatically refreshing, pre-calculated views
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Queries can get complex and can take longer to run than a user loading a dashboard would like to wait. For popular queries used frequently in dashboards or alerting and detection rules, it pays to accelerate them by pre-calculating the results, like we do with Transforms. Views can store a query for easy reuse, and by automatically running the view on a recurring basis, Materialized Views make them faster.

**Expected outcome**

Address the critical problem of slow dashboards and analytical queries over large datasets. Pre-calculate and store the results of resource-intensive ES|QL queries, with a mechanism to automatically keep the stored data refreshed. This transforms query performance from minutes to seconds, improving the user experience for Security and Observability use cases.

**Key Capabilities:**

Implement CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_name ... AS ... syntax.
Include a REFRESH policy clause (e.g., REFRESH EVERY 5 MINUTES) to control data freshness.
Develop a background process to automatically execute refreshes according to the policy.
Store pre-calculated results in a managed Elasticsearch index.
Provide management commands like SHOW MATERIALIZED VIEWS, DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW, and ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW (to change refresh policy).
Handle refresh failures and provide monitoring capabilities for refresh status and resource cost.

summary.value: Accelerates threat detection and mission-critical dashboards from minutes to seconds, strengthening cyber defense posture and supporting responsive, data-driven decisions across IL4/IL5 classified and FedRAMP-authorized environments
summary.scope: Pre-calculated, auto-refreshing ES|QL query views with configurable refresh policies, managed storage, lifecycle commands, failure handling, and refresh monitoring

items.title: Faster and more efficient vector rescoring: Direct I/O vector rescoring
items.body: Currently rescoring in vector search is done off-heap. This usually impacts the page cache and increases the variance in query latency (compared to the potential performance if it’s done on disk). It also makes it more difficult to calculate RAM needs and the impact of sizing on query latency. Rescoring vectors can be specified for all HNSW index types, including bbq_hnsw (the current default) and bbq_disk (which we expect to be our future default), so it is important. Using direct I/O for rescoring will perform the rescoring on disk and refrain from polluting off-heap memory and impacting caching. In other words, users will likely experience it as lower query latency and lower variance in query latency.

summary.scope: Direct I/O vector rescoring for HNSW index types (including bbq_hnsw and bbq_disk) that reduces memory contention and query latency variance in vector search operations

items.title: Strong identity verification for remote clusters
items.body:
**What the feature is and who it’s for**
When configuring a remote cluster for CCS/CCR operations, cross-cluster API keys are created on the cluster that owns the data, and then they are deployed to the cluster that performs the remote query.
These keys can be leaked during the initial transfer or exposed in misconfigured logs. Attackers can leverage a leaked cross-cluster API key to get unauthorized access to data, since there is no way to validate if the source is the intended one or if the request is coming from a malicious system.

**Value proposition**
We want to implement strong identity verification for remote connections doing CCS/CCR operations.
In addition to the cross-cluster API key, the cluster initiating the connection will be required to prove its identity (e.g. with a TLS certificate). These two credentials are strictly related, so that one can only be used with the other. For example, the API key can be signed with the TLS certificate at runtime when the remote request is performed.

This enhanced security feature will increase the security posture of remote connections, that are a critical path for our Security and Observability customers.

fieldValues.Title: Strong identity verification for remote clusters

summary.for: DOD and defense program managers, data owners, and cybersecurity officers operating multi-cluster or distributed Elastic environments requiring zero-trust and data access controls
summary.value: Eliminates unauthorized cross-cluster data access risk by binding API keys to verified TLS identities, strengthening zero-trust posture and supporting IL4/IL5 and CMMC credential integrity requirements
summary.scope: Dual-credential enforcement for remote cluster operations—API keys cryptographically tied to TLS certificates to prevent key theft, replay attacks, and spoofed cluster access

items.title: Analyze nanosecond precision log data with ES|QL
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Date nanoseconds is the default timestamp format for OTel. 8.18 made date nanos field type support GA in ES|QL. But if a query's data contained both date nanos and regular date types in the same field, the query would fail or return null. This would be expected for a data stream that has transitioned its timestamp from date to date nanos, and could break existing dashboards. We want to make it easier to migrate to date nanoseconds format.

**Expected Outcomes**

In 9.2, ES|QL will automatically cast the dates to date nanos so the query can return results and preserve the higher precision. This will be useful when working with datastreams that started with date type and then migrated to date nanos, or when querying multiple data sets that have the two different date types.

fieldValues.Title: Analyze nanosecond precision log data with ES|QL

summary.for: DOD and federal agency security operations teams and mission owners running OTel-based observability or SIEM pipelines requiring high-fidelity event correlation
summary.value: Ensures uninterrupted log analysis and dashboard continuity during data stream migrations, reducing risk of visibility gaps critical to threat detection and compliance audit trails
summary.scope: ES|QL auto-casting of mixed date/date-nanosecond fields, enabling seamless querying across legacy and nanosecond-precision OTel data streams without query failures or null results

items.title: Advanced AI Inference in ES|QL: Reranking, Text Generation, and Embeddings
items.body:
**What the feature is**
Advanced AI Inference in ES|QL: RERANK Command, COMPLETION Command, and TEXT_EMBEDDING Function.

**Value proposition**
Users creating search and generative AI applications often struggle to fully integrate machine learning inference into their data and query pipelines. This typically requires separate tools or complex setup to use advanced models for tasks like re-ordering search results, creating content, or performing semantic search. This feature places advanced AI functions directly within ES|QL. This provides built-in commands and functions for reranking search results, generating text, and creating text embeddings using ES|QL's simple, piped syntax. This deep integration makes it much easier to build intelligent applications and improves the quality of search results and content creation efficiency for all users.

**Expected behaviors**
A reranking command will be available within ES|QL to re-order documents from a query based on a trained relevance model, leading to better search result quality.
A completion command will be available within ES|QL to generate different types of text and content, such as summaries or answers, using integrated language models.
Completion and reranking should work by default with our Elastic managed LLM that's available in EIS. This means users don't have to preconfigure any endpoint, they just connect to EIS (and we do this already for them if they are using Elastic Serverless or Elastic cloud hosted).
A text embedding function will be available within ES|QL to convert text into high-quality vector representations, enabling direct use in vector search and data analysis workflows.

**Key user stories / use cases**
As a search engineer, I need to use the ES|QL reranking command to apply a custom model to improve the accuracy of my e-commerce product search results after the initial retrieval step.
As an application developer, I want to use the ES|QL completion command to quickly create a summary of a document directly in an ES|QL query result set.
As a security analyst, I want to use ES|QL completion to assign scores to events depending on whether they represent a malicious action or not.
As a data scientist, I need to use the ES|QL text embedding function to vectorize millions of customer reviews directly within my data preparation query before storing the vectors for semantic search.

fieldValues.Title: Advanced AI Inference in ES|QL: Reranking, Text Generation, and Embeddings

summary.for: DOD and IC data engineers, mission application developers, and security operations analysts building AI-enabled search and analytics pipelines on Elastic
summary.value: Embeds AI inference natively into query workflows—enabling semantic search, automated threat scoring, and intelligent summarization without external ML tooling, reducing attack surface and keeping sensitive data within compliant Elastic environments
summary.scope: ES|QL RERANK command, COMPLETION command (with Elastic-managed LLM), and TEXT_EMBEDDING function for vector search, all operable within existing Elastic pipelines

items.title: ES|QL queries can return partial results when they encounter issues
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Distributed systems must be resilient to real world operational issues. A shard or node might be unavailable during a query, or one remote cluster times out while the others return results quickly. Most users we've talked to prefer to receive partial results, clearly indicated, rather than a failed search or query.

**Expected outcomes**

Introduce a new allow_partial_results setting, which is enabled by default. If a shard is temporarily unavailable, your ES|QL query will now succeed with results from all available shards rather than failing completely.
Similar to search DSL, if ES|QL encounters a shard issue, it can now continue the query and return as many results as possible, as well as an indicator that the results are partial.

fieldValues.Title: ES|QL queries can return partial results when they encounter issues

summary.for: DOD and federal agency mission operators and data engineers running analytics across distributed or multi-cluster environments
summary.value: Ensures mission-critical queries continue returning actionable intelligence even during node failures or network disruptions, supporting operational resilience in degraded or contested environments
summary.scope: New allow_partial_results setting for ES|QL that returns available data with partial-result indicators when shards or remote clusters are unavailable, enabled by default

items.title: Random sampling in ES|QL to balance speed and accuracy
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Sometimes you want to aggregate a large dataset quickly but you don't need a 100% accurate result. If you're willing to tradeoff speed for accuracy, sampling can be used to reduce the volume of data that needs to be processed, while still providing usable results.

**Expected outcomes**

Accelerated query performance by reducing data volume in aggregations and visualizations, especially on large datasets. A sampling capability in ES|QL enables fast data exploration of data without the need to process the entire index. Maintains statistically meaningful insights by balancing speed and accuracy.

fieldValues.Title: Random sampling in ES|QL to balance speed and accuracy

summary.for: Defense analysts and intelligence mission owners managing high-volume data environments requiring rapid situational awareness
summary.value: Accelerates threat hunting and large-scale log analysis across massive datasets, enabling faster operational decisions without degrading analytical utility
summary.scope: Random sampling in ES|QL queries that reduces data processing volume while preserving statistically meaningful aggregations and visualizations

items.title: Logical Views: Users can define and reuse named query definitions
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Data teams frequently struggle with long and complex queries that are difficult to maintain, share, and reuse. This complexity leads to inconsistencies, errors, and wasted time, creating a roadblock to valuable insights. Critical logic for dashboards, reports, and interactive analyses often gets buried in lengthy queries, making them challenging to maintain, share, and adapt. Analysts tell us they struggle with "overly long queries." This leads to time wasted deciphering logic and risks not only inconsistencies but also the use of unvetted, inefficient queries that can impact quality.

**Expected Outcomes**

This outcome addresses the user problem of managing long, complex, and repetitive ES|QL queries. Users can encapsulate complex logic into a named, reusable query definition (a "view"). This simplifies query writing, improves maintainability, and ensures consistency across teams, directly addressing pain points described by our users. This feature provides syntactic abstraction but does not store data or improve performance of the underlying query (that's materialised views).

**Key Capabilities:**

- Implement CRUD APIs to define and manage logical views.
- Allow users to query the view directly in the FROM clause (e.g., FROM my_view | ...).
- Integrate views with RBAC for create, query, and manage permissions.
- Ensure Kibana can discover views for features like autocomplete.

fieldValues.Title: Logical Views: Users can define and reuse named query definitions

summary.for: Defense data analysts, mission intelligence teams, and program managers overseeing multi-source data environments requiring consistent, auditable query logic
summary.value: Enforces standardized, vetted query definitions across teams—reducing analytic inconsistency and supporting auditability and access control alignment critical to DOD data governance and compliance requirements
summary.scope: Named, reusable ES|QL query definitions (logical views) with CRUD management APIs, RBAC-integrated permissions, and native Kibana discovery/autocomplete support

items.title: Enabling Direct Data Migration with Reindex from Remote for Serverless
items.body:
**Description:**
To provide greater feature parity with other Elastic deployment models and address a key requirement for data mobility, we are enabling the **Reindex from Remote** API for Elastic Serverless. This feature delivers a direct, server-side mechanism for moving data from a source Elasticsearch cluster into a Serverless project.

As a well-established Elasticsearch capability, Reindex from Remote offers a flexible method for operators to selectively copy indices or data streams and supports using ingest pipelines for in-flight data transformations. This API is intended as a foundational building block for technical users building migration workflows. 

**Key Capabilities:**

*   **Direct, Server-Side Data Transfer:** The destination Serverless project connects directly to the source cluster to pull data, eliminating the need for an intermediary client to handle the data transfer.
*   **Flexible Data Transformation:** Leverage ingest pipelines during the reindex process to modify, enrich, or restructure documents as they are moved, ensuring data conforms to the destination schema.
*   **Secure Intra-Cloud Connectivity:** For this release, the feature is scoped to support data sources within the Elastic Cloud ecosystem. This includes Elastic Cloud Hosted (ECH) and other Elastic Serverless projects, regardless of cloud provider or region. Support for sources outside of Elastic Cloud may be considered in the future.

items.title: Search flattened fields in ES|QL to avoid mapping explosion
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Elasticsearch's flattened field type is sometimes used for indexing objects with a large or unknown number of unique keys, such as AWS Cloudtrail logs. This avoids mapping explosion by having one field where there might otherwise be tens of thousands of fields. ES|QL does not yet support the flattened field type.

**Expected Outcome**

Users will be able to retrieve fields from within flattened fields in ES|QL queries, which enables them to filter on the field values, aggregate them, and apply other ES|QL features. This unlocks analytics on these fields that was previously not possible with ES|QL.

items.title: Reduce Log Storage Costs with Transparent Pattern-Based Compression
items.body:
Reduce Log Storage Costs with Pattern-based Compression for log messages (LogsDB) 

## Value Prop

LogsDB is composed of various optimizations that provide up to 3.2x storage savings for storing logs. Even after all those savings, in most log datasets the message field relatively uses most of the storage compared to other fields. This is because log messages are stored as is and general compression doesn’t work well on them. However each log message typically originates from a log pattern in some system or application and is very repetitive. Our goal is to extract log patterns from log messages and store the pattern separately from the arguments (e.g. timestamp, host names, etc.), which should result to compressing log messages very efficiently.

## Expected Outcome

A new field type, `pattern_text`, is introduced to replace `match_only_text` for LogsDB. The transparent log pattern matching and  templating mechanism will offer ~50% log message storage reduction and a ~13% additional overall storage improvement on top of LogsDB with all the Enterprise features enabled. 

Also, introduce a new setting that will optionally enable positions to be stored for faster phrase queries at the expense of the gained storage. The end result is similar storage as LogsDB without `pattern_text` for a significant boost on phrase queries

Licensing: Enterprise
- Pattern-based compression depends on synthetic source (Enterprise feature) to work properly and complements it. 
- In case of a license downgrade, the storage saving features of `pattern_text` will be disabled after the next rollover. Upgrading back to Enterprise will similarly enable back the storage saving features of `pattern_text` on the backing indexes created after the next rollover.
 
We plan to release `pattern_text` as a Tech Preview feature with Elasticsearch 9.2 and in GA with Elasticsearch 9.3

Key Capabilities:
- Implement the new `pattern_text` field type in LogsDB.
- Develop the ingestion-time logic to automatically identify and separate log templates from arguments.
- Implement the query-time logic to seamlessly reconstruct the original log message for full-text search queries.
- Ensure the feature is enabled via a simple change in the index mapping.
- Validate storage savings and minimal performance overhead on indexing and search.

items.title: Process very large data and result sets with ES|QL
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Users with large datasets sometimes need to return more rows from a query than ES|QL allows. We're currently constrained to the amount of available Java heap memory as we lack features like Spill to Disk. Without pagination, increasing the LIMIT just creates a larger response object putting more stress on the network and the client, such as Kibana. We need to be able to return results in pages (say, 10 at a time for a Search results web page) without forcing a rerun of the same query.

Also, as much as we love Kibana, not all analysis happens there. Sometimes you need to share a large number of rows from a query with an external party (auditor, federal agency) that doesn't have access to your cluster. Users have asked to be able to process and export hundreds of thousands, even millions, of rows from a query.

**Expected Outcomes**

Features like pagination and spill to disk would enable ES|QL to handle and return much larger working sets, enabling higher LIMITs and larger exports to external systems. Pagination facilitates efficiently browsing through large result sets in Kibana Discover and in search use cases.

items.title: ES|QL subquery: Enable multi-phased analytics with query within a query
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

​​Analysts and engineers striving for deep, actionable insights often need to perform complex data correlations and multi-stage investigations. Their analytical process is frequently iterative; the results of one exploratory query often dictate the parameters or focus of subsequent queries. This requires a way to chain analytical steps, using dynamically generated lists or conditions from one phase of an investigation to inform the next, a common pattern when hunting for security threats, diagnosing complex system failures, or building sophisticated detection logic. Without the ability to easily express such nuanced relationships and sequential logic within their primary query language, analysts face friction and delays in reaching critical conclusions.

**Expected Outcome**

New levels of analytics become possible within a single query, as results from an inner "subquery" can be passed to an outer query. This enables complex, multi-stage analytical logic to be expressed concisely and executed efficiently. For example, craft a single ES|QL query to find all failed authentications only for users who recently accessed a critical system identified by a subquery.

items.title: ES|QL: Lookup on more flexible criteria than single exact match
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Lookup Join introduced the ability to do SQL-style LEFT OUTER JOINs in ES|QL. The initial release of Lookup Join relies on a common field name between the left index (from the source command) and the right index (the lookup index), and an exact match of the field values. This satisfies many use cases but more flexibility is needed for other use cases. Users need to match on multiple fields, more complex criteria like < or >, combine multiple criteria with AND and OR logic, and even full text function matching.

**Expected outcome**

ES|QL joins can satisfy a broader set of use cases by offering more expressive join predicates such as <, >, !=, multi-field matches, AND and OR logic, and  full text function matching. This can also reduce the need for altering the data schema or using workarounds like renaming and concatentation.

items.title: Serverless users can search across multiple projects
items.body:
**Value proposition**

Cross-Project Search (CPS) is a foundational feature reimagined for the Elastic Serverless platform, enabling Enterprise customers to seamlessly unify data scattered across multiple projects. This capability is critical for architectural requirements such as data residency, scalability, cost optimization, and tenant isolation. 

The core value propositions delivered by CPS are:
1. Unified, "Flat-World" Search Experience: Searches initiated from an origin project automatically span all configured linked projects by default. This eliminates the need for users to modify every search request or manually manage project identifiers in queries.
2. Simplified Configuration: Configuration is streamlined to a single-step process in the Cloud UI/API, avoiding the complex, error-prone certificate, IP address, and API key management required by traditional Cross-Cluster Search (CCS).
3. Consistent and Secure Data Access: Utilizing the new Universal Identity and Access Management (UIAM) model, CPS ensures that access permissions to data are consistent, regardless of whether the user accesses the data directly from the linked project or via the origin project (the core tenant of the new security model).
4. Cross-Solution Analysis: CPS supports linking Observability (O11y) and Security projects, enabling use cases such as analyzing O11y logs to find security threats, capturing the value of cross-solution capabilities.

**Expected outcome**

The initial phase of Cross-Project Search (CPS) will deliver foundational capabilities that redefine unified data access across multiple Elastic Serverless projects:
1. Unified, "Flat-World" Search Experience:
    ◦ The system will introduce a "flat-world" mental model where remote data is treated identically to local data for search operations.
    ◦ Searches initiated from an origin project will span all configured linked projects by default, eliminating the need for users to prefix queries with project names.
    ◦ Unqualified index expressions (e.g., FROM foo) will implicitly refer to that resource on the origin project and all linked projects that contain a matching resource.
    ◦ The architecture will actively prevent cross-project chaining in this initial phase; searches will only span the origin project and its directly linked projects.
2. Simplified Configuration and Management:
    ◦ A streamlined, centralized interface (Cloud UI/API) will be delivered for linking projects, replacing the multi-step, error-prone configuration (certificates, API keys) required by traditional Cross-Cluster Search (CCS).
    ◦ Configuration information will be propagated securely to Elasticsearch clusters using established Kubernetes configuration patterns, allowing Elastic to hot-reload link configurations without service interruption.
    ◦ Administrators can define linked projects using project names or metadata attributes, but only a fixed list of project IDs will be saved initially.
    ◦ Configuration will be strictly unidirectional: configuring Project A to search Project B does not automatically enable Project B to search Project A.
3. Consistent and Secure Access Control (UIAM):
    ◦ The implementation will leverage the new Universal Identity and Access Management (UIAM) security model.
    ◦ Access permissions to data will be enforced consistently, regardless of whether the user accesses the data directly from the linked project or via the origin project .
    ◦ Support for Universal API keys will enable client applications and critical Kibana background tasks (like alerting rules) to search across configured projects programmatically using a single, multi-project credential.

items.title: ES|QL name qualifiers to manage duplicated column names in a query
items.body:
Name qualifiers

Value Proposition

As ES|QL gains more ways to analyse and correlate multiple datasets in one query, like joins, views, and subqueries, there becomes a need to resolve or prevent field name conflicts and ambiguities. For example, a lookup index used with Lookup Join might have fields with the same names as fields in the source index of FROM. A subquery could return fields conflicting with field names in the surrounding query. Adjusting the schema of the datasets won't always be a good option - Users need to be able to handle these scenarios within the query. RENAME is sometimes an option but it can be cumbersome, especially when there are many fields involved. 

Expected Outcomes

Users will be able to assign names (qualify) to the source indices, lookup indices etc within a query, and refer to those names elsewhere in that query to disambiguate columns. This is commonly available in databases as "aliasing" or name qualifiers. Users will be able to disambiguate between main fields and lookup fields in joins, avoid name conflicts, and queries written using qualifiers in FROM and LOOKUP JOIN will remain stable even in case of growing schemas.

An example query might look as follows (exact syntax TBD):
```
FROM main_idx AS main
| LOOKUP JOIN lu_idx AS lookup ON [main].[field] == [lookup].[field] AND [main].[field2] > [lookup].[other_field], ... | KEEP [lookup].[field]

```

items.title: Preserve input columns when calculating STATS (INLINE STATS)
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

The STATS command only outputs the aggregations that you ask it for - it drops the input fields. Some use cases for the STATS command need to do another operation on the original fields afterwards but can't as those field are gone. You might want to compare an individual row's data with the aggregated data for the group it belongs to, e.g. comparing a per-group COUNT computed via STATS to the total count over all groups. We need a command that can add aggregations onto rows without removing the input columns.

**Expected Outcomes**

INLINE STATS provides aggregation capabilities like STATS, but without dropping the input columns. After calculating the STATS, the results will be joined back onto the respective rows as new columns, preserving the original columns.

INLINE STATS has GA'ed in 9.3 - [docs](https://www.elastic.co/docs/reference/query-languages/esql/commands/inlinestats-by)

items.title: ES|QL Lookup Join functionality equivalent to Enrich for usage at query time
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

ES|QL added support for join-like operations early on with ENRICH, as it was convenient to reuse the existing ENRICH infrastructure from ingest pipelines. However, as ENRICH was optimised for ingest time lookups and small datasets that don’t change often, it has a poor UX with many commonly reported limitations when it is used for query time lookups as in ES|QL. To address this we introduced Lookup Join, which has advantages over ENRICH (lookup indices are directly updatable without enrich policy execution, lookup join supports complex expression joins...) but still has some feature gaps (lookup join doesn't support date/IP/numeric range matches like ENRICH, etc). This puts the burden on users to understand whether they should use ENRICH or LOOKUP JOIN in their queries. The goal is to achieve feature parity to remove that burden.

**Expected Outcome**

Lookup join will have feature parity with enrich, making Lookup Join the clear choice and removing user confusion about which feature to use. As we invest in Lookup Join enhancements and optimisations, we're also building a stronger foundation for additional types of joins in the future.

items.title: Replace proprietary RUM Agent with new OTel-based Javascript SDK
items.body:
**Value proposition**
This feature will provide an Elastic Distribution of OpenTelemetry version of the [OpenTelemetry JavaScript SDK](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/languages/js/), providing the ability to use the OpenTelemetry JavaScript SDK to provide RUM instrumentation data to Elastic Observability. The capabilities targeted for the initial work are to as closely match the data types that enable the user outcomes that can be achieved today with our existing proprietary agent/solution for real user data.

The Elastic Distributions of OpenTelemetry (EDOT) have been developed to enhance the capabilities of standard OpenTelemetry distributions and improve existing OpenTelemetry support from Elastic. 

EDOT are composed of OpenTelemetry (OTel) project components, OTel Collector, and language SDKs,  which provide users with the necessary capabilities and out-of-the-box configurations, enabling quick and effortless infra and application monitoring.

While OTel components are feature-rich, enhancements through the community can take time. Additionally, support is left up to the community or individual users and organizations. Hence EDOT will bring the following to end users:

- Deliver enhanced features earlier than OTel: By providing features unavailable in the “vanilla” OpenTelemetry components, we can quickly meet customers’ requirements while still providing an OpenTelemetry native and vendor-agnostic instrumentation for their applications. Elastic will continuously upstream these enhanced features.

- Enhanced OTel support - By maintaining Elastic distributions, we can better support customers with enhancements and fixes outside of the OTel release cycles. In addition, Elastic support can troubleshoot issues on the EDOT.

**Expected outcome**
- Users will have clear documentation for configuring the OpenTelemetry Javascript SDK to send data to Elastic Observability (for all deployment types: self-managed, Elastic Cloud Hosted, and Elastic Cloud Serverless)
- Content pack will provide Kibana dashboards, alerting and SLOs to leverage OTel JS SDK data
- OTel JS SDK will be enhanced to include Core Web Vitals, error capture as well as RUM metadata enrichment covering device type, OS Family & Version, Browser Family & Version and location (Country/Region/City)
- Full EDOT JavaScript SDK will be available

items.title: Simplified search in ES|QL editors
items.body:
**What the feature is**

Simplified Search in ES|QL editors

**Value proposition**

Analysts, support engineers, and SREs who prefer simple search inputs often find ES|QL syntax too technical for quick searches. This Simplified Search provides an effortless, KQL-like "type and go" experience that auto-generates valid ES|QL queries. By removing the need to learn complex syntax for basic searches , users can get immediate, usable results and smoothly transition to deeper analysis using the full ES|QL editor. 

**Expected outcome**

* Type and Go Search: Users can enter simple free text (e.g., error, host1) into a single input field without worrying about ES|QL syntax.
* Automatic Query Translation: The system automatically translates the simple input into a valid ES|QL query. For example, a free text query like "disk full" is translated using the KQL() function.
* Seamless Query Evolution: Users can immediately see the query produced by the simple search and easily move to the full ES|QL editor to refine or expand their search without starting over.
* KQL Carryover: When switching from KQL to the simplified ES|QL mode, the original KQL expression (e.g., host: cdn) will be shown in the new input field, and the ES|QL editor will reflect the correctly translated query.

items.title: Azure Private Link support in Elastic Cloud Serverless
items.body:
**What the feature is and who it's for:** This is a new capability allowing customers to establish secure, private network connections to their Serverless projects, using Azure Private Link, to ensure data traffic bypasses the public internet. This feature is designed for enterprise customers, particularly those in regulated industries or with strict corporate security policies that prohibit data traversal over public networks. Cloud administrators can configure these private endpoints and use VPC filtering to control access from specific virtual private clouds. The setup is managed directly within the Elastic Cloud console for a seamless administrative experience.

**Value proposition:** The primary value of this feature is a significantly enhanced security and compliance posture for sensitive workloads running on Elastic's serverless platform. By eliminating public internet exposure, it drastically reduces the attack surface and helps organizations maintain a zero-trust network architecture. This capability unblocks the adoption of Elastic Cloud Serverless for enterprises that were previously hesitant due to stringent data governance requirements. It allows organizations to fully leverage the operational efficiency and scalability of a serverless architecture without compromising on enterprise-grade security. Ultimately, this solidifies Elastic Cloud Serverless as a secure-by-design platform, empowering customers to confidently run their most critical applications in the cloud.

items.title: Read-only dashboards sharing
items.body:
**What the feature is (as Title)** Read-only dashboards sharing

**What the feature is and who it’s for**
Users want to build their dashboards and share them with colleagues without the risk that someone unintentionally modifies them. Today, all users with write access to a given Space can change any dashboard in it, and this approach doesn't really fit scenarios where more consistency is needed.

**Value proposition**
We want to introduce a new concept of write-protected dashboard, where only the owner of the dashboard can modify it. All other users with proper privileges can access these write-protected dashboards, but they will not be allowed to change anything.

This is just a first step in the direction of sharing dashboards between users in a more granular way, where collaboration is still possible without requiring Space isolation and duplicates.

**Expected outcome**

items.title: Time-series support in ES|QL (Tech Preview)
items.body:
**External Title**
Time-series support in ES|QL (Tech Preview)  - Advanced time series analytics

**Value Prop**
In Elasticsearch 9.2 we introduced as a Tech Preview the [TS command](https://www.elastic.co/docs/reference/query-languages/esql/commands/ts) to query time series data streams and an initial set of [time series aggregation functions](https://www.elastic.co/docs/reference/query-languages/esql/functions-operators/time-series-aggregation-functions).

Our goal is to enable more time-series use cases; we'll extend time-series support in ES|QL by providing more types of operations and aggregation functions used in more advanced time-series analytics.

**Expected Outcome**

Extend time-series support in ES|QL
- Additional time series operations and aggregation functions
  - { percentile, stddev, variance}_over_time
    Calculate the {percentile, standard deviation or variance} over time of a field
  - deriv (simple linear regression)
    Calculates the derivative over time of a numeric field using linear regression
  - clamp (field, min, max)
    Limits (or clamps) the values of all samples to have a lower limit of min and an upper limit of max
  - trange
    Filter data for the given time range using the `@timestamp` attribute
- Advanced label processing
  - efficient prefix/wildcard filtering on dimensions
  - STATS BY ALL
    When no dimension is provided on the BY clause, the query automatically groups by all dimensions available
- Window functions that allow for lookback periods to smooth results over specific periods
   e.g. "STATS rate(requests, **5m**) BY host, TBUCKET(1m)"
  Initially release with window as a multiple of tbucket, with future releases supporting windows that are smaller or not a multiple of tbucket
- Exponential histogram field support in ESQL (new type of explicit histogram that will also be introduced)

items.title: Improve downsampling - Full ESQL support and coverage of counters and histograms
items.body:
## External Title

Improve downsampling - Full ESQL support and coverage of counters and histograms

## Value Prop

Metrics tools and solutions collect large amounts of time series data over time. As the data ages, it becomes less relevant to the current state of the system. Downsampling reduces the storage footprint of time-series data by storing it at reduced granularity, while, at the same time, queries over larger spans of time that do not need the accuracy provided by raw data touch less data and can be much faster.

The focus of downsampling in Elasticsearch has been mainly on the storage reduction side up to now. We want to enable a more complete experience for downsampling and querying data. We'll start by offering full ES|QL support for accessing and querying downsampled data, together with options for downsampling all important types of metrics (gauges, counters and histograms).

## Expected Outcome

- Support all ESQL functions over downsampled data 
  - Value: A downsampled index can be used in place of the raw index for all queries
- Store deltas for counters to calculate rates accurately
  - Value: downsampling will have no impact on counter rate accuracy, in line with min/max/sum/count/avg on gauges
- Add support for downsampling histogram fields
  - Value: Histograms are used extensively in metrics, this covers an important gap in supporting the OTel data model and increasing applicability of downsampling.

fieldValues.Title: Improve downsampling - Full ESQL support and coverage of counters and histograms

summary.value: Reduces long-term metrics storage costs and query latency without sacrificing data accuracy, supporting leaner, faster mission system observability within constrained or classified environments
summary.scope: Full ES|QL query support over downsampled data, accurate counter rate preservation, and histogram field downsampling aligned with OpenTelemetry data models

items.title: TSDB++ - Improve storage efficiency, ingestion throughput and query latency
items.body:
## External Title

Improve performance for storing and managing metrics data (TSDB)

## Value Prop

Observability teams rely on metrics to monitor system health and diagnose production incidents. The high volume of this data forces a trade-off between storage costs, data completeness, and the speed of analysis during an outage. This means teams often have to limit monitoring to critical services or reduce data retention periods, creating blind spots that can prolong investigations.

This initiative breaks that compromise. We are changing the cost-performance of time series data streams (TSDB), Elastic's solution for storing metrics data, enabling teams to ingest significantly higher volumes of metrics and retain them for longer without a proportional increase in hardware costs. This allows for comprehensive, real-time monitoring of an entire estate, leading to faster problem detection and more accurate capacity planning.

## Expected Outcome

Make TSDB a more performant offering for storing and managing metrics data. Our plan is to significantly improve overall storage efficiency and ingestion throughput, while further improving the query performance of select queries that take more than 100ms.

- TSDB storage used for common metrics use cases reduced by 30% in Elasticsearch 9.3 and up to 3x (70%) in Elasticsearch 9,4
- TSDB indexing throughput for common metrics use cases increased by up to 2x

items.title: Improve LogsDB storage efficiency and query performance
items.body:
## External Title

Improve performance for storing and querying logs data (LogsDB)

## Value Prop

Logs are essential for observability, providing the detailed context needed for root cause analysis. However, their high volume and unstructured nature make them disproportionately expensive to store and slow to query. This forces teams to choose between retaining complete data for investigations and managing escalating storage costs, often slowing down critical troubleshooting during an outage.

This initiative directly addresses this trade-off. By fundamentally re-architecting how log data is stored, we will significantly reduce the storage footprint and cost associated with high-volume logs. This allows teams to retain more data for longer and enables them to query that data faster, accelerating investigations and reducing system downtime.

## Expected Outcome

LogsDB, Elastic's solution for storing logs, is composed of various optimizations that provide up to 3.2x storage savings for storing logs and efficient log specific operations. We plan to keep on pushing towards even better scale, performance & cost-effectiveness for storing, querying, and managing logs.

As a first step, we are going to focus on building the fundamental internal structures required to provide up to 2x additional storage savings and ingestion throughput in the future. While doing so, we'll introduce smaller scale efficiency improvements that will benefit specific use cases like storing wildcard and multi-valued fields (IPs, MACs, etc), or string fields with many unique values (high-cardinality fields).

Further improve LogsDB performance, mostly focusing on storage efficiency, which is crucial for the logs use cases:
- Introduce internal structures that will enable Elasticsearch to store data only once by default
  - Foundational work with the long term goal to be able to store data once as doc values (internal columnar store), with no fall back to stored fields or additional indices
- Introduce Storage efficiency improvements for the following field types
  - High-cardinality string fields (string fields with many unique values)
  - Wildcard and multi-valued fields (IPs, MACs, etc)
  - Use lightweight doc-value skippers (skip lists) in favor of indexed data structures for some types of fields 
- Query performance improvements
  - Improved hashing performance in the compute engine (ES|QL)
  - Amortized dictionary lookups for low cardinality fields

items.title: Faster and more efficient vector rescoring: Bulk vector rescoring
items.body: Currently, rescoring in vector search is done by fetching and rescoring each vector separately, which is slower and requires more calls to the memory/disk. This slows query latency and increases resource consumption, and is less suitable for work with object store when the data is not cached. Rescoring vectors can be specified for all quantized HNSW index types, including bbq_hnsw (the current default) and bbq_disk (which we expect to be our future default), so it is important. Using bulk fetching for rescoring adds prefetches to rescoring operations so that the data is read into the heap all at once, then rescored, which reduces computing memory consumption and improves query latency, in particular if the data is not cached in serverless.

summary.value: Faster, lower-latency vector search with reduced compute resource consumption supports mission-critical AI applications in cost-constrained, serverless, or object-store environments aligned to IL4/IL5 deployment patterns
summary.scope: Bulk prefetch-based vector rescoring across all quantized HNSW index types (bbq_hnsw, bbq_disk) reducing memory overhead and improving query speed especially for uncached or serverless data

items.title: Improve vector ingest and indexing performance
items.body: To be able to search data, first one needs to get the data into the system and organize it in a structure that can be searched efficiently. That part is typically referred to as ingest and indexing. It is the first interaction any user has with the system, but it is also an ongoing part of the system operation and potentially consumes computing resources. Ingest and indexing performance is important, because it impacts users in scenarios ranging from the time they need to wait for the data to be searchable when introducing a new model that generates new vectors, to the potential increase in query latency when computing resources are being allocated to indexing in the background when data is added or updated on a regular basis. Indexing dense vectors is often an operation that requires substantial computing resources. In recent months ingest and indexing performance was improved by tens of percents, in particular with the abilty to ingest binary format that reduces dense vector ingest time to around half of the original duration.

items.title: Blend relevance signals with Hybrid Search in ES|QL with FORK and FUSE
items.body:
**What the feature is**
Simple Hybrid Search in ES|QL using the FORK and FUSE commands, enabling Reranking with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) and Linear Combination.

**Value proposition**
Users currently struggle to combine the results from different types of searches—like keyword-based full-text search and vector-based semantic search—into a single, unified, and highly relevant result set. This often requires running separate queries and merging the results externally, which adds complexity and latency. The FORK and FUSE commands in ES|QL solve this by allowing users to run multiple search commands (e.g., MATCH and KNN) concurrently and then combine their ranked outcomes using state-of-the-art fusion algorithms like RRF or Linear Combination. This provides a simple, direct, and more accurate way to perform hybrid search, significantly improving result quality for users who need to blend different relevance signals.

**Expected behaviors**
The FORK command will allow users to execute two or more distinct search-related ES|QL commands (e.g., MATCH and KNN) independently within a single query.
The FUSE command will be used to combine the ranked results from the FORK branches.
The FUSE command will support Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) as a method for combining the rankings from the different searches.
The FUSE command will support Linear Combination as a method for combining the final scores from the different searches.
Users will be able to perform advanced hybrid search without needing to write complex, multi-stage application logic.

**Key user stories / use cases**
As a search engineer, I need to use the FORK and FUSE commands with RRF to combine the results of a traditional keyword search and a modern vector search, ensuring the final result set benefits from both relevance methods.
As an application developer, I want to use the FORK and FUSE commands with Linear Combination to perform a simple hybrid search where I can explicitly set weights for full-text and semantic scores to quickly tune result relevance.
As a data analyst, I need to run a complex ES|QL query that uses WHERE clauses on metadata and then uses FORK and FUSE to get the best possible hybrid-ranked output.

fieldValues.Title: Blend relevance signals with Hybrid Search in ES|QL with FORK and FUSE

summary.scope: ES|QL FORK and FUSE commands enabling concurrent keyword and vector search with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) and Linear Combination reranking in a single, unified query

items.title: Reranking using 3rd party rerankers
items.body:
**External Product Outcome**
Often a semantic model that allows very accurate ranking comes at a large cost in performance, resources, or both. It is impractical to use such a model to scan a large index, but it is often ideal to scan a large index using a more economic method, obtain the top ranking documents, and then rerank them using the accurate (though costly) model, because running it on a small number of documents is quite economic. We now expose the ability to rerank that way, using a 3rd party reranker in ES|QL.

summary.scope: ES|QL integration enabling two-stage search where large indexes are scanned efficiently, then top results are reranked using high-accuracy third-party reranker models

Running Calculations: Users will be able to compute running sums, counts, averages, and other aggregate measures per event, over a specified window or group.
Temporal Trend Identification: Observe how metrics evolve over time for individual entities or across event sequences, assisting in identifying patterns or deviations.
Contextual Monitoring: Add event-specific statistics that aid in monitoring system behavior, user activity, or process performance by showing how current events relate to recent history.
Support for Anomaly Detection: Provide additional data points on each event, such as a rolling average or standard deviation, which can be used to identify unusual occurrences.
Preservation of Event Detail: Perform statistical analysis without aggregating away the original individual events, allowing for deeper investigation.

items.title: Serverless Backup & Restore
items.body:
**What the feature is and who it's for**
Elastic Cloud Serverless Backup is a user-facing backup and restore experience for Elastic Cloud Serverless projects (Search, Observability, and Security). You get a list of backup points by date and time, can restore from any point you choose (not only the latest), and over time can configure schedules and retention, run backups on demand, and optionally send backups to your own object store (AWS S3, GCP, or Azure). There are no snapshot APIs or repository names to manage, just a Backup section in the Cloud UI and a simple Backup API. The feature is for teams running production search, observability, or security workloads on Serverless who need reliable recovery from mistakes, compliance-friendly retention, and the ability to clone or migrate data from a backup.

**Value proposition, for the user**
You get visibility and control over your backups without dealing with low-level snapshot or repository configuration. You can see when backups were taken, choose which point in time to restore from (e.g. before an accidental delete or bad deploy), and - as we roll out phases—set schedule and retention, run a backup on demand, and optionally store backups in your own CSP object store for governance. Restore starts in the same project (to new indices or, when supported, in place); later we may add restore to another project and creating a new project from a backup. The experience is the same whether backups live in Elastic-managed storage or your own CSP object store: one Backup API and one UI, so you can recover with confidence and meet “backup included” expectations without managing infrastructure.

fieldValues.Title: Serverless Backup & Restore

summary.for: Government and defense IT program managers, cloud architects, and compliance officers running production workloads on Elastic Cloud Serverless
summary.value: Enables auditable, point-in-time data recovery with customer-controlled storage (AWS S3, GCP, Azure) to support data sovereignty, accidental-deletion recovery, and compliance-friendly retention requirements for FedRAMP and IL-aligned environments
summary.scope: UI and API-driven backup and restore for Serverless Search, Observability, and Security projects—including selectable restore points, on-demand backups, configurable schedules and retention, and bring-your-own object store support

items.title: Search Management Dashboard in Kibana
items.body:
**What the feature is and who it's for:**
Administrators often face challenges in identifying and managing queries that take longer to execute and can impact cluster performance. Additionally it is hard to track the source of those queries and who submitted them. Currently, locating these queries and acting upon them requires correlating multiple sources including the tasks API and query logs.

**Expected outcome**
The Search Management Dashboard is designed for these admins. It provides a centralized interface in Kibana that highlights long-running queries, making it easier for admins to identify and manage queries that could impact cluster performance. Currently, these capabilities are available in low-level tasks API. The new dashboard makes it simpler and more accessible to monitor and manage running queries.
Admins should be able to list running queries and see key details about each, like how long they've been running and who started them. They should be able to easily cancel a query from this list. This will give our admin users the tools they need to maintain optimal cluster health, peak search performance and quickly identify and resolve query inefficiencies.

AutoOps for air-gapped environments is an advanced monitoring, diagnostics, and optimization solution built for administrators running the Elastic Stack in secure, isolated environments, including Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK), Elastic Cloud Enterprise (ECE), or bare-metal installations. It is designed specifically for organizations with strict regulatory or security requirements, such as large-scale federal, government, and banking institutions who cannot send metrics to Elastic Cloud. The solution is deployed entirely within the customer's own environment, ensuring all analysis and metrics are performed and stored locally.

**Value proposition**
This new capability enhances AutoOps to support isolated environments, a capability that up until now was only available for Elastic Cloud Hosted or via Cloud Connected that require sending metrics to the cloud. This provides access to advanced, actionable insights within a secure, disconnected environment. Unlike other tools that only provide metrics, AutoOps correlates data to deliver insights, highlight potential causes for issues, and offer clear recommendations. This enables technical teams to perform guided troubleshooting, optimize resource utilization, and identify misconfigurations, ultimately reducing the time and expert resources required to manage large-scale Elastic deployments. All analysis is performed locally, ensuring no customer data leaves the secure network.

items.body:
**What the feature is and who it’s for**
Elastic Inference Service (EIS) is now available to self-managed Elasticsearch deployments through Cloud Connect. EIS provides access to Elastic-managed LLM, vector generation, and rerank models running on fast, GPU-based infrastructure. It powers advanced capabilities such as RAG, semantic search, and the AI Assistants in Elastic’s Search, Observability, and Security solutions.

This release is designed for enterprise administrators, developers, and data teams running Elasticsearch on their own infrastructure who want to use high-performance, managed AI models without provisioning GPU hardware or managing complex third-party ML stacks. Through Cloud Connect, self-managed clusters connect securely to Elastic Cloud, automatically creating inference endpoints that integrate directly into existing workflows.

**Value proposition**
Self-managed Elasticsearch users can now run advanced AI-powered features with the same speed, scalability, and ease of use as Elastic Cloud customers. EIS removes the need to deploy and maintain GPU-accelerated ML nodes locally, reducing infrastructure costs and complexity. It offers predictable, per-token pricing and scales seamlessly from prototype to production.

By enabling EIS through Cloud Connect, organizations gain unified visibility and governance for connected clusters. Inference endpoints are automatically provisioned, enabling immediate use of vector search, semantic search, and AI Assistants with Elastic’s full suite of managed models.

items.title: Improved AI Suggestions for Stream Partitioning and Processing
items.body:
### What the feature is

Improved AI Suggestions for Stream Partitioning and Processing

---

### Value proposition

Users onboarding new data often struggle with the manual effort required to partition streams and define complex processing rules like Grok patterns. Existing AI suggestions help, but can be overly specific, don't cover the full pipeline (like date parsing), or are inflexible, forcing users to make manual corrections after accepting. This initiative will enhance our AI capabilities to provide more flexible and comprehensive assistance. Users will benefit from smarter, more generalized Grok and Dissect patterns, suggestions for entire multi-step ingest pipelines, and the ability to review and refine all AI-generated suggestions before saving them.

---

### Expected behaviors

* Provide AI-generated suggestions for `dissect` processors, in addition to existing `grok` suggestions.
* Improve AI-generated `grok` patterns to be more general and less overly specific (e.g., by better handling whitespace and generic data fields).
* Suggest entire multi-step processing pipelines (e.g., Grok + Date + Remove processors) rather than just a single processor.
* Allow users to review, edit (e.g., change the name or condition), and refine AI-generated partition suggestions before accepting them.

---

### Key user stories / use cases

* As a user, when my log message contains a custom timestamp, I want the AI to suggest not only the `grok` pattern to extract it, but also the `date` processor needed to parse it and set it as the `@timestamp`.
* As a user, when my logs have minor variations, I want the AI to generate a `grok` pattern that is general enough to handle all variations, rather than a brittle pattern that only matches a few samples.
* As a user, when my data is delimited (like CSV or space-delimited), I want the AI to suggest a `dissect` pattern to parse it.
* As a user, when the AI suggests a partition named `logs.trader-logs`, I want to edit the name to `logs.trader` before I accept the suggestion.
