items.title: Intelligent Asset Suggestions and AI Generation
items.body:
### Value proposition:

When users add data to a stream, they often face a "blank slate" problem, requiring manual effort to build relevant dashboards, processing rules, and alerts. This feature provides an AI-powered assistant to solve this. It will proactively analyze data and make deterministic suggestions from a knowledge base (e.g., "This looks like Nginx data, apply this dashboard?"). Furthermore, it will introduce generative AI workflows to create new assets, either based on the system's understanding of the data or by allowing users to ask for new content in natural language. This dramatically accelerates time-to-value, helping all users get insights from their data immediately.

### Expected behaviors:

* **Deterministic Suggestions:** Automatically analyze data sent to a stream and suggest relevant, pre-built assets (like processing logic, dashboards, or alert rules) from an Elastic knowledge base.
* **Proactive AI Generation:** Proactively generate new, suggested assets (like a starter dashboard) based on the system's automated understanding of the data's structure and content.
* **Reactive AI Generation:** Provide generative AI workflows that allow users to create new assets (like visualizations or processing rules) using natural language prompts.
* All generative AI features will be clearly marked as "Preview."

### Key user stories / use cases:

* **As an SRE,** when I send new AWS logs to a stream, the system recognizes the data and suggests, "We recommend applying the standard AWS S3 dashboard and processing rules."
* **As an engineer,** I send custom application logs to a new stream. The system analyzes the fields and proactively generates a "starter" dashboard showing a timeline of events and a table of the most common fields.
* **As an analyst,** I am viewing a stream and I use a prompt to ask the AI, "Build a pie chart showing the distribution of `http.status_code` and a bar chart of the top 10 `user.name`."

items.title: Logs Streams
items.body:
### Value proposition:

Observability, security, and platform engineers need a simple, consistent, and scalable way to manage their log data. Today, this often requires manually configuring and maintaining complex index templates, ingest pipelines, and retention policies across many disparate data sources, which is error-prone and time-consuming. 

Logs Streams provide an opinionated, managed, and hierarchical system for all logs. This allows users to easily organize data (e.g., `logs` $\to$ `logs.production` $\to$ `logs.production.api`), with settings like processing, schema, and retention being automatically inherited by sub-streams. This dramatically simplifies configuration, ensures consistency, and provides a single, queryable entity for all your log data.

### Expected behaviors:

* **Hierarchical Organization:** Organize all logs under a single, high-level `logs` stream and create "Ingest Sub Streams" (e.g., `logs.production.api`) to partition data.
* **Setting Inheritance:** Define ingest processing, mappings, and retention policies on a parent stream (e.g., `logs.production`), and all its sub-streams will automatically inherit these settings.
* **Automatic Normalization:** Incoming log data is automatically processed and normalized to an OTel-like shape, ensuring consistent field names and types out-of-the-box.
* **Centralized Management:** Manage the full lifecycle of your logs. From ingest processing to schema and retention, in one place.

summary.scope: Hierarchical log organization with inherited retention, processing, and schema settings, automatic OTel normalization, and centralized lifecycle management across all log streams

items.title: Query Streams and Draft Mode
items.body:
### Value proposition:

Users need more flexibility in how they organize, view, and process their data. Sometimes they need to create custom, reusable views that span multiple data sources (e.g., "all production errors"), or filter a single source. 

Other times, they need to safely test new ingest-time processing and partitioning rules without risking production data. This feature introduces two powerful capabilities: 
- Query Streams let users define new virtual, read-only streams using ES|QL
- Draft Mode provides a way to test and preview new ingest logic at query-time before making it permanent.

### Expected behaviors:

This feature is composed of two related functionalities:

**1. Query Streams (Virtual Data Views)**
* Define and create a new, queryable stream using a free-form **ES|QL query**.
* Create "standalone" streams to unify data from multiple sources (e.g., `FROM logs.nginx, logs.k8s.nginx*`).
* Create "sub-streams" to create a filtered, read-only view of an existing stream (e.g., `FROM my_stream WHERE status_code > 400`).
* These streams are **virtual and read-only**; they do not store data and resolve the query at read time.
* Attach assets like dashboards and alerts directly to these virtual streams once available in Streams (outside of this initial feature).

**2. Draft Mode (Safe Processing Testing)**
* Create new Log Sub Streams in a new **"Draft Mode."**
* Define partitioning and processing rules in this safe "sandbox" environment.
* This logic is **applied at query time**, not at ingest time, allowing you to preview the results on live data without any permanent impact.
* Validate your new processing logic (e.g., in dashboards) to ensure it's correct.
* Once confident, **"Promote"** the draft rules to make them permanent, which applies them to the live ingest pipeline.

items.title: Entity Analytics - Watchlist
items.body:
**Background:**

The Watchlist feature provides a way for analysts to manually tag and monitor high-risk entities. Besides alerts, security teams often have known risks they must monitor. These can include privilege administrators, C-level executives (high value targets), Departing Employers (potential insider threats), or concerning behaviors from machines or AI Agents.

**Value proposition**

Our watchlists serve as the ideal starting point for hypothesis-driven threat hunting. Instead of boiling the ocean, hunters can begin with a pre-curated, risk-scored list of entities that already warrant suspicion (e.g., "Privilege users", "Departing Employees", or "Unauthorized 3rdparty LLM Usage by Employees."). By reviewing the detailed behavioral baselines and anomalies for just this high-risk group, hunters can efficiently uncover sophisticated, low-and-slow attacks that would otherwise remain invisible.

items.title: Entity Analytics - Entity Graph
items.body:
**Problem Statement**
Security investigations are often slow, siloed, and event-centric. Analysts must manually pivot between disparate logs (process, network, authentication, etc) to piece together an attacker's actions. This makes it difficult to quickly determine the full scope (blast radius), root cause, relationships and attack path of a security incident.

**Value proposition**

items.title: Browser locale language support in Kibana
items.body:
**What the feature is**
Browser Locale Language allows users to view the Kibana interface in their preferred language (French, German, Japanese, Chinese, or English) by automatically detecting and applying the setting configured in their web browser, without having to configure a .yml file. 

**Value Proposition**
Currently, global organizations are forced to default all users to English, despite having employees across multiple countries and languages. Users on Serverless cannot change their language setting. This creates a significant usability gap and makes Kibana less accessible and less competitive compared to multi-language competitors like Grafana and Splunk. By automatically detecting the browser locale, Kibana offers a personalized experience that improves adoption may make it easier for some customers to migrate from on-premises to Elastic Cloud and Serverless.

**Expected Outcome**
- Kibana will automatically detect the user's browser-set preferred language on first load and apply the corresponding language (e.g., zh-CN, ja-JP, fr-FR).
- Users can override the language setting with a UI control
- For on-premise users, this UI setting will override the default language set in kibana.yml, enabling multi-language support even within a single on-prem deployment.

**Key User Stories / Use Cases**
User Story: "As a Japanese Analyst on Serverless, I want Kibana to automatically appear in Japanese when I log in, without having to ask my administrator to change a server configuration file I don't have access to."
User Story: "As a Global Administrator of an ECH deployment, I want users in Germany and France to be able to use Kibana in their respective languages without me having to manage separate deployment instances."

items.title: Late Chunking support for JinaAI embeddings
items.body:
## Value proposition

Enable higher-relevance retrieval on long documents by adding Late Chunking support for JinaAI embeddings to the Inference API. Unlike naïve chunking -which splits first, embeds each chunk, then pools- Late Chunking embeds the full document first and then chunks based on the embedded tokens.

This ordering preserves significantly more cross-chunk context, improving semantic continuity and reducing "lost signal". The result is measurable relevance gains on long-form content, especially for documents in the 1,500+ token range, where standard chunking may degrade search quality.

## Expected outcome  

Inference API Late Chunking support for JinaAI embeddings.

**Value proposition**
Developers and SREs who run agentic workloads on Bedrock AgentCore want to be able to understand the performance, reliability, cost and output quality. This enables them to monitor the health of their agentic workloads and have the necessary observability for quicker root cause analysis during operational issues. AI agentic applications leverage complex workflows between LLMs, agents and tools. LLMs are opaque and non-determining systems which could have unpredictable output, cost and performance spikes. Users need an evolved layer on top of traditional observability to be able to address these needs.

Elastic LLM Observability for agentic AI applications enables users to observe the AgentCore platform in which their workloads run, as well as get detailed information on the execution path of their applications for easy troubleshooting. They get curated set of metrics and logs with pre-configured out of the box dashboards. To enable root cause analysis, Elastic APM visualizes the flow of execution with information such as tokens, latency, LLMs, agents, tool calls, etc. 

**Expected outcome**

- Out of the box dashboards (as part of the integration) for the vended metrics and logs

**What the feature is (as Title)** Enhanced Insight and Usability of OOTB Dashboards for popular off-the-shelf services
We are reimagining the OOTB dashboards for popular off-the-shelf (OTS) services, to deliver enhanced insight and a superior user experience. Our focus is on better usability, navigability, structure based on key use cases, and visual consistency. This ensures users can quickly answer their most frequent questions, understand service health, and effortlessly troubleshoot operational issues by seeing the most important data upfront.

**Value proposition**
We will provide SREs and developers using OTS services with a superior, purpose-built OOTB dashboard experience that turns complex operational data into immediate, actionable insight, allowing them to troubleshoot faster and manage service health with confidence. 

**Expected outcome**

1. Task-Oriented Navigation and Discovery
Users will be able to easily find information depending on the task at hand. The dashboards will be organized around common use cases and component-centric views, dramatically reducing search time and cognitive load.

2. Effortless Troubleshooting with Context
Users will be able to follow a clear path to resolution using contextual navigation. They will be able to find related information with intuitive drill-downs that move logically from high-level summaries (like overall service health) to granular details (like specific sub-components) without losing context.

3. Rapid Data Filtering and Focus
Improved usability of filters to necessary data only with smart, context-aware filters. This enables users to instantly focus on specific namespaces, workloads, or timeframes, accelerating analysis and bypassing data clutter.

4. Confident Operational Decisions
Ultimately, this combination of structure, clarity, and rapid access to information ensures users can quickly and accurately answer their most frequent questions. This leads to greater confidence in managing service health and effortless troubleshooting of operational issues.

items.title: Best-Practice OOTB Alerts for popular off-the-shelf services with Elastic Integrations
items.body:
**What the feature is (as Title)** Best-Practice OOTB Alerts for popular off-the-shelf services with Elastic Integrations
Elastic OOTB alert rule templates enable users to set monitoring coverage for their most popular off the shelf services, with minimum investment and expertise needed. 

**Value proposition**
***- User & Problem:***
SREs and developers who rely on critical Elastic-monitored services (e.g., AWS S3, NGINX, PostgreSQL) face significant time sink and risk when setting up monitoring for them. They must invest considerable time researching, configuring, and manually maintaining bespoke alert rules, often missing critical signals or violating service-level objectives (SLOs) due to the scattered and non-standardized nature of "best practice" documentation.

***- Proposed Solution:***
Elastic Curated Best-Practice Alert Templates for Integrations. This feature embeds expert knowledge directly into the platform, providing pre-configured, production-ready alert rule templates for key signals across popular off-the-shelf services (e.g., AWS S3, NGINX, PostgreSQL). The templates are automatically deployed and updated as the underlying Elastic Integration evolves.:***

**Expected outcome**
***- Key Benefits & Value:***

Eliminate Alert Research: Instantly create alerts for critical signals (e.g., NGINX 5xx errors, high PostgreSQL query latency, AWS S3 throttling) with minimal research time.

**Accelerate Time-to-Value:** Move from deploying an Integration to having full, production-grade alerting coverage in minutes, not days.

**Guaranteed Relevance:** Alert templates are maintained by Elastic, ensuring template rules are automatically updated to match new service versions, telemetry changes, or evolving best practices. Users will be able to easily update rules when templates change, with minimal effort.

**Reduce MTTD:** Enable early visibility into critical threshold breaches, allowing teams to solve operational issues before they escalate into high-severity outages. 

***- Key jobs-to-be-done:***
This capability enables customers to solve the following customer goals, questions and problems:
1. What are the recommended alerts for the services I'm monitoring with the integrations (eg AWS S3, NGINX, PostgreSQL, etc)?
2. What are the recommended configurations?
3. Why are you recommending them?
4. Some rule threshold and other config are context specific (for example, CPU utilization). What guidelines should I use to tweak the thresholds?
5. I want to easily copy the alert rule config so I can create my alerts
6. When I'm still in the planning phase, I want to be able to see the recommended alerts for the services I want to monitor. I don't want to have to onboard an integration just so I can see the recommended alerts. I want to see all of them in the documentation and be able to copy the rule (either from the documentation or from a link in github). I don't want to have to log in unnecessarily.

items.title: Alert Close with Reason
items.body:
# **Alert Close with Reason**

## **Value proposition**

To streamline the alert triage process, we will enhance the alert closure workflow. Currently, analysts complete their investigation or response by closing an alert and can optionally add tags or notes to document their findings. While helpful, this existing method is not a streamlined solution.

By requiring analysts to select a "reason" when closing an alert, we can capture critical metrics to improve team performance and operations. For example, consistently tracking "false positive" as a close reason allows the detection engineering team to directly identify and refine problematic detection rules. This standardized approach ensures that valuable information about the alert's disposition is systematically stored.

## **Expected outcome**

Users require the ability to close an alert and provide a summary reason for the closure. Initially, these reasons will not be customizable, but users will expect customization, similar to the existing alert tagging workflow. The process of defining a close reason must also be accessible via APIs to support automation workflows. This reason will be stored in a dedicated field within the alert document, specifically `kibana.alert.workflow_reason`. While this added information allows users to create custom visualizations, the goal is to enhance default experiences in the future.

fieldValues.Title: Alert Close with Reason

summary.for: SOC managers, cyber defenders, and compliance officers in DOD/defense agencies requiring auditable, standardized incident response workflows
summary.value: Enforces structured alert disposition tracking to support audit trails, RMF compliance, and detection rule refinement—reducing false positives and strengthening mission-critical security operations
summary.scope: Mandatory close-reason selection on alert triage, API-accessible workflow reason field, and structured data storage for reporting and automation integration

items.title: New Attack Viewer for Context Pivoting
items.body:
## New Attack Viewer for Context Pivoting

### Value proposition

Analyst workflows currently include a dedicated experience for viewing attacks, which is effective for immediate response. However, this approach limits the ability to easily cross-reference and preview attack details within other relevant contexts. For example, when an analyst is viewing an alert associated with an attack, they are currently forced to navigate to a separate page.

To streamline this process, we plan to enable a more flexible workflow by introducing a dedicated attack view, similar to the existing alert view. This will allow users to preview attack information directly within their current context, eliminating the need to pivot between applications and facilitating a more integrated user experience.

### Expected outcome

When interacting with indexed data (documents or simply, rows in a table), users expect the ability to view the selected content in a separate, portable view while keeping the primary data table accessible.

Similar to the established alert experience, where alerts are managed and viewed on a dedicated page, users expect a consistent interaction pattern for attacks, which are stored in the same manner. This expectation extends beyond merely viewing attacks like alerts; users also need to reference attacks from various other contexts, including alerts, events, entities, and cases.

items.title: New Detection Overview Dashboard
items.body:
## New Detection Overview Dashboard

### Value proposition

As experiences evolve to improve the triage of attacks and alerts, users require a clear detections overview dashboard within the Security Solution. This dashboard will summarize critical detections and provide users with key performance indicators (KPIs) related to all alert and attack data.

Key features may include:
* **Alert and Attack Metrics**: Summaries of critical alerts, including team assignment breakdowns, alert severity, and response times.
* **False Positive Analysis**: Utilization of the new alert close reason feature to incorporate metrics on why detections are being closed, such as tracking false positive rates and their improvement over time.
* **Comparative Analytics**: Core functionality focused on time-based comparisons, enabling teams to understand if they are experiencing a higher impact from alerts today than previously.
* **AI-Powered Guidance**: Integration of generative AI to provide summarized context and actionable guidance on the next steps for users.

### Expected outcome

Create a single, central dashboard for Security Operations Center (SOC) leads and teams to monitor and understand their alert and attack response performance. This should provide a summarized view for a quick, high-level summary of response effectiveness. There should be the presence of standard metrics to track and display critical performance indicators such as Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and Mean Time to Respond (MTTR). Trend analysis should show performance trends over time to facilitate continuous improvement and content needs to be actionable so users can pivot from the dashboard to relevant, detailed content for deeper investigation.

items.title: ES|QL FORK command
items.body:
**What the feature is**

The `FORK` processing command creates multiple execution branches to operate on the same input data and combines the results in a single output table.

In this quarter, we aim to move `FORK` from tech preview to GA.

**Value proposition**

Users can run multiple queries, differentiate, mutate, and merge them together. This can enable such search use cases such as [hybrid search with custom result fusion](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/hybrid-search-multi-stage-retrieval-esql), useful for cases where you need to bring in results from different indices or retrieval types.

```
FROM books METADATA _score, _id, _index
| FORK (WHERE KNN(description_vector, ?query_vector) | SORT _score DESC | LIMIT 100)
       (WHERE MATCH(description, ?query) | SORT _score DESC | LIMIT 100)
| FUSE // uses RRF by default
| SORT _score DESC
```

**Expected outcome**

We plan to make this General Availability.

fieldValues.Title: ES|QL FORK command

summary.value: Enables simultaneous multi-index querying and result fusion within a FedRAMP-eligible platform, accelerating threat detection, intelligence correlation, and mission data analysis across disparate sources
summary.scope: GA release of ES|QL FORK command supporting parallel execution branches, hybrid search with custom result fusion, and unified output from multiple query strategies

items.title: Remove limitations on full text functions in ES|QL
items.body:
**What the feature is**

Full text functions cannot be currently used in ES|QL after pipeline breakers or on runtime columns. We will lift both of these restrictions.

**Value proposition**

This change will reduce user friction and enable advanced search capabilities not available in query DSL via search on runtime columns. By allowing for full text functions after pipeline breakers, we enable actions like rescoring, and by allowing for search on runtime columns, users can go beyond keyword matching on these columns, but instead search on full text.

Example of a full text function after a pipeline breaker (`LIMIT`):

```
FROM books
| WHERE title:?query
| SORT title DESC
| LIMIT 100
| WHERE description:?query
```

Example of a full text function on a runtime column (syntax not finalized):

```
FROM books METDATA _score
| STATS languages = VALUES(language),
        titles = VALUES(title),
        rating = MAX(rating) BY code
| SORT rating DESC
| LIMIT 100
| WHERE match(titles, "Harry Potter")
        OR match(titles, "Harry Potter", { "index_analyzer": "my_analyzer"})
```

**Expected outcome**

Users can use full text functions after pipeline breakers or on runtime columns in ES|QL.

items.title: AI Agent monitoring and evaluation
items.body:
**What we are building*
This provides a solution to effectively monitor and evaluate AI Agents created using Elastic Agent Builder. 
- Visualize traces of the agent conversation including tool calls, search results, and generated outputs 
- Receive metrics on the performance of retrieval steps in the Agent based on LLM-as-a-judge evaluators 
- View key Agent metrics like groundedness, factuality, and hallucination rates and track over time 
- Extend with the ability to add custom evaluators or connect to third party evaluators 

**Value proposition**
These capabilities will allow users to build confidence in the performance of their Agents and move more quickly from development to production

items.title: ES|QL: Lookup Join on wildcard match
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Lookup Join introduced the ability to do SQL-style LEFT OUTER JOINs in ES|QL. The initial release of Lookup Join relies on a common field name between the left index (from the source command) and the right index (the lookup index), and an exact match of the field values. This satisfies many use cases but more flexibility is needed for other use cases. Some users need to match on wildcards.

**Expected outcome**

ES|QL joins can satisfy a broader set of use cases by offering more expressive join predicates including wildcards. This can also reduce the need for altering the data schema or using workarounds.

items.title: ES|QL: Lookup on more flexible criteria than single exact match
items.body:
**Value Proposition**

Lookup Join introduced the ability to do SQL-style LEFT OUTER JOINs in ES|QL. The initial release of Lookup Join relies on a common field name between the left index (from the source command) and the right index (the lookup index), and an exact match of the field values. This satisfies many use cases but more flexibility is needed for other use cases. Users need to match on multiple fields, more complex criteria like < or >, combine multiple criteria with AND and OR logic, and even full text function matching.

**Expected outcome**

ES|QL joins can satisfy a broader set of use cases by offering more expressive join predicates such as <, >, !=, multi-field matches, AND and OR logic, and  full text function matching. This can also reduce the need for altering the data schema or using workarounds like renaming and concatentation.

items.title: SIEM Readiness: Visibility Health & Data Coverage
items.body:
**SIEM Readiness: Visibility Health & Data Coverage**

**Value proposition** 

Elastic Security users ingest logs, enable rules, and deploy agents — but today there is no single place to answer: "Is my SIEM actually ready to detect threats?"

SOC teams maintain spreadsheets, build custom dashboards, and manually reconcile data sources against detection rules — only to discover gaps during audits or live incidents.

SIEM Readiness introduces a centralized, actionable health view inside Elastic Security that continuously evaluates whether you have the right data, in the right shape, with sufficient reliability and retention to support your active detections.

**Expected outcome**

A new SIEM Readiness page in Elastic Security that provides at-a-glance health across four dimensions:

**Coverage** — Are the data sources your detection rules depend on actually present? Which log categories (Endpoint, Identity, Network, Cloud, Application/SaaS) are covered vs. missing?
**Quality** — Are your logs ECS-compatible? Surface data sources with field mapping issues that silently break rules, dashboards, and correlations.
**Continuity** — Are your ingest pipelines healthy? Detect pipeline failures before they create blind spots.
**Retention** — Do your lifecycle policies meet industry benchmarks (FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001)? Flag categories falling short of recommended retention windows.
Every signal is tied to a guided action: view affected integrations, investigate in Discover, adjust policies, or generate a prefilled case to assign remediation to the right team.

**Optional: Key user stories / use cases**

- As a SOC Manager, I want a single readiness view that shows whether my SIEM has the right data for my detections, so I can report operational posture to leadership without maintaining spreadsheets.

- As a Detection Engineer, I want to see which enabled rules are missing required data sources (mapped by MITRE ATT&CK tactic), so I can prioritize onboarding the data that matters most.

- As a Platform Engineer, I want to know which ingest pipelines are failing and which data streams have ECS incompatibilities, so I can fix issues before they impact detections.

- As a Compliance Manager, I want to verify that log retention meets regulatory benchmarks across all categories, so I can prepare for audits without manual data collection.

summary.value: Continuously validates that detection coverage, log quality, pipeline health, and retention policies meet compliance benchmarks—reducing audit risk, closing blind spots before incidents, and providing defensible posture reporting to leadership
summary.scope: Centralized SIEM Readiness dashboard covering data source coverage by MITRE ATT&CK tactic, ECS field-mapping quality, ingest pipeline continuity alerts, and retention compliance checks against FedRAMP/SOC 2/ISO 27001 with guided remediation actions

items.title: ES|QL support in Grafana Elasticsearch plugin
items.body:
## Context and Value Proposition
Grafana [Elasticsearch plugin](https://grafana.com/grafana/plugins/elasticsearch/) is one of the most used plugins with Grafana. ES|QL is Elastic’s powerful query language and query engine that supports piped queries, rich support for aggregations, text processing, filtering, lookup joins, trace analytics, and comprehensive time series analytics. The lack of ES|QL support in the plugin makes it harder for users to perform those analytics in a standardized way in Grafana. We would like to offer customers the ability to execute ES|QL queries in the Grafana plugin.

## Outcomes
Enhance the plugin to support the ability to run ES|QL queries in Grafana UI on an Elasticsearch backend, and retrieve and see the results in Grafana. 
provide the following capabilities:
* Base ability to execute a well formed ES|QL, get results back, and visualize in Grafana UI
* Syntax highlighting
* AST
With these capabilities, uses will successfully be able to analyze, monitor, and visualize their data including logs, metrics, and traces, via the powerful ES|QL language in Grafana.

summary.value: Enables unified, standardized analytics across logs, metrics, and traces within existing Grafana workflows—strengthening operational visibility and supporting security monitoring requirements for classified and mission-critical environments
summary.scope: ES|QL query execution in the Grafana Elasticsearch plugin with results visualization, syntax highlighting, and AST support for logs, metrics, and trace analytics
